german_sentence: Der Schlüssel liegt auf dem Tisch.
english_sentence: The key is on the table.

extra_examples.english: I can already see the bus.
She helps the new neighbor.
The children are playing in the garden.

usage_note: The definite article agrees with gender, number, and case. Learn nouns with their nominative article, but expect the article to change in a sentence.

extra_examples.german: Der Kaffee ist heiß und stark.
extra_examples.english: The coffee is hot and strong.

extra_examples.german: Wir öffnen das Fenster und warten.
extra_examples.english: We open the window and wait.
Jonas and Lea live here.

usage_note: Und joins words, phrases, or main clauses without changing normal main-clause word order.

english_sentence: The milk is in the refrigerator.

extra_examples.english: We are going into the kitchen.

extra_examples.german: Im Sommer schwimmt sie oft im See.
extra_examples.english: In summer she often swims in the lake.

extra_examples.german: Er legt die Karte in den Umschlag.
extra_examples.english: He puts the card into the envelope.

usage_note: In takes dative for a location and accusative for movement toward a destination. Im means in dem; ins means in das.

forms.forms: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind
war, warst, war, waren, wart, waren

extra_examples.german: Wir sind seit Montag in Berlin.
extra_examples.english: We have been in Berlin since Monday.

extra_examples.german: Als Kind war ich sehr ruhig.
extra_examples.english: As a child, I was very quiet.
The train has already departed.

usage_note: Sein is highly irregular. It also forms the perfect tense of many verbs of motion or change of state.

german_sentence: Vor dem Haus steht ein Fahrrad.
english_sentence: There is a bicycle in front of the house.

extra_examples.english: Nora orders a tea.
He is speaking with a colleague.
One of the windows is open.

usage_note: Ein has no plural article form. Its endings follow the same pattern as possessives such as mein and dein; alone, it can mean one.

forms.forms: habe, hast, hat, haben, habt, haben
hatte, hattest, hatte, hatten, hattet, hatten

extra_examples.english: Do you have class today?
Lina had a headache yesterday.

extra_examples.german: Ich habe die Rechnung schon bezahlt.
extra_examples.english: I have already paid the bill.

usage_note: Haben expresses possession and many common conditions. It forms the perfect tense of most German verbs.

german_sentence: Klara kommt später; sie arbeitet noch.
english_sentence: Klara is coming later; she is still working.

extra_examples.german: Ich treffe sie vor dem Kino.
extra_examples.english: I am meeting her in front of the movie theater.

extra_examples.german: Die Gäste sind da, aber sie warten draußen.
extra_examples.english: The guests are here, but they are waiting outside.
Could you please help me?

usage_note: Lowercase sie can mean she, her, they, or them. Capitalized Sie means formal you and takes plural verb forms.

forms.forms: werde, wirst, wird, werden, werdet, werden
wurde, wurdest, wurde, wurden, wurdet, wurden

english_sentence: In the afternoon it suddenly gets cold.

extra_examples.english: I will call you tomorrow.
The road is being closed today.

extra_examples.german: Aus dem Setzling ist ein kräftiger Baum geworden.
extra_examples.english: The seedling has grown into a strong tree.

usage_note: Werden means become; with an infinitive it can mark future, and with a past participle it forms the event passive.

german_sentence: Der Brief ist von meiner Tante.
english_sentence: The letter is from my aunt.

extra_examples.german: Wir fahren vom Bahnhof mit dem Bus weiter.
extra_examples.english: We continue from the train station by bus.

extra_examples.german: Das Ende von dem Film war überraschend.
extra_examples.english: The ending of the film was surprising.

extra_examples.german: Das Fenster wurde von einem Handwerker repariert.
extra_examples.english: The window was repaired by a tradesperson.

usage_note: Von always takes dative. It can mark origin, an of-relationship, or the agent in a passive sentence; von dem normally contracts to vom.

extra_examples.german: Heute koche ich für meine Freunde.
extra_examples.english: Today I am cooking for my friends.
Can you hear me?
I am a little cold.

usage_note: Ich is nominative and is not capitalized unless it begins a sentence. Use mich for accusative objects and mir for dative objects.
